attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health check. She has recently moved to the area and this is her first appointment at your practice. She has a medical history of type 2 diabetes diagnosed five years ago and mild asthma. Her regular medications include metformin and a salbutamol inhaler as needed. She works as a teacher at a local primary school.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 27 kg/m2.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate approach to identifying whether this patient is of Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander origin?
options: Ask all patients in a standardised way as part of routine demographic data collection
Ask only if you suspect the patient may be Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander based on appearance
Ask indirectly by enquiring about eligibility for Indigenous-specific health services
Avoid asking unless the patient volunteers this information
Check previous medical records for this information without asking the patient
attributes.explanation:
Identifying Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander status is an important aspect of providing culturally appropriate healthcare and ensuring access to specific health programs and services designed to address health disparities. The most appropriate approach is to ask all patients in a standardised way as part of routine demographic data collection.

Ask all patients in a standardised way as part of routine demographic data collection is the correct approach. This ensures that identification is consistent, non-discriminatory, and becomes a normal part of the registration process for all patients. The recommended question is: "Are you of Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander origin?" This approach aligns with national guidelines and avoids making assumptions based on appearance or other factors.

Ask only if you suspect the patient may be Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander based on appearance is inappropriate and potentially discriminatory. Making assumptions based on physical appearance is unreliable and can be offensive. This approach would lead to inconsistent identification and perpetuate stereotypes.

Check previous medical records for this information without asking the patient is not best practice. While reviewing previous records is important, a person's cultural identity is self-determined, and their identification status may have changed. Additionally, previous records may be incomplete or inaccurate. Direct confirmation from the patient is always preferable.

Ask indirectly by enquiring about eligibility for Indigenous-specific health services is an indirect approach that may make patients uncomfortable or confused. It could be perceived as questioning their legitimacy to access services rather than respectfully acknowledging their identity.

Avoid asking unless the patient volunteers this information will result in significant under-identification. Many Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people will not volunteer this information without being asked, particularly if they have experienced discrimination in healthcare settings previously.

Proper identification enables healthcare providers to offer culturally appropriate care, including access to specific health programs such as Indigenous health checks (Medicare Benefits Schedule item 715), the Closing the Gap Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme Co-payment Measure, and other targeted health initiatives. It also contributes to accurate population health data, which is essential for monitoring health outcomes and developing effective health policies.
learningPoints: Identifying Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander status is essential for providing culturally appropriate care and accessing specific health services.
Asking all patients about their Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander status in a respectful manner is best practice.
Self-identification is the only appropriate method for determining Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander status.
references: Identification of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people in Australian general practice. RACGP position statement. July 2020. <https://www.racgp.org.au/getmedia/02e43a67-73e3-4321-a3eb-b3a8d77b6910/RACGP-position-Identification-in-General-Practice.pdf.aspx>

Morgan S, Thomson A, O'Mara P, Tapley A, Henderson K, van Driel M, Scott J, Spike N, McArthur L, Magin P. Identification of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander status by general practice registrars: Confidence and associations. Aust Fam Physician. 2016 Sep;45(9):677-82. PMID: 27606373. <https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2016/september/identification-of-aboriginal-and-torres-strait-2>
